# Unify Table — Complete Documentation

> DuckDB-native data table & charts library for React. SQL does the work — sort, filter, group, aggregate all happen in DuckDB-WASM. 16 composable plugins, 7 display types, zero-config to fully headless.

Source: https://unify.sh/unify-table

---

# Getting Started

Build your first DuckDB-powered data table in under 5 minutes.

## Prerequisites

Before you start, make sure you have:

- **React 19+** as your UI framework

- **A DuckDB-WASM connection** that
implements the `TableConnection` interface — see
the [Query Engine](#/query-engine) page
for setup details

- **pnpm, npm, or yarn** as your package manager

## Installation

The library is split into two packages you install directly: **core** (SQL
engine and data source) and **react** (components,
plugins, and charts). The charts package is included as a dependency of react — no separate install needed.

**Terminal**
```bash
pnpm add @unify/table-core @unify/table-react
```

## CSS Imports

Unify Table ships its own CSS rather than relying on Tailwind. Add these three imports to your
app entry point. They cover panel layouts, theme class definitions, and display renderer styles
(charts, stats, pivots).

**main.tsx**
```tsx
import '@unify/table-react/styles';    // Panel layouts + base component CSS
import '@unify/table-react/themes';    // Dark & light theme class definitions
import '@unify/table-react/displays';  // Display renderer CSS (charts, stats, pivots)
```

## Your First Table

The simplest possible table requires just two props — a DuckDB connection and a table name:

```tsx
<Table db={db} table="trades" />
```

That's it. The Table component auto-detects columns from DuckDB, virtualizes rows for
performance, and handles scrolling. No column definitions needed — DuckDB's schema IS the
source of truth. Here it is running live:

> **Tip: Column auto-detection** When you omit the `columns` prop, the table queries DuckDB
for `DESCRIBE` metadata and builds column definitions automatically. You can
always pass explicit `columns` for fine-grained control over labels,
widths, formatting, and sort behavior.

## Adding Interactivity

The basic table above is read-only. To add features, compose plugins — each one is an
independent module. Start with the table you just built and layer on capabilities:

```tsx
import { Table, filters, selection, columnResize, darkTheme } from '@unify/table-react';

<Table
db={db}
table="trades"
plugins={[filters(), selection('multi'), columnResize()]}
styles={darkTheme.styles}
height={500}
/>
```

Here's what each plugin in the example above does:

- **filters()** — adds column-level filter
dropdowns with composable predicates (equals, contains, greater than, etc.)

- **selection('multi')** — enables cell
selection with single-click, Shift+click range, and Ctrl+click multi-select

- **columnResize()** — adds drag handles on
column borders for adjusting widths

There are 16 plugins in total, covering everything from inline editing and clipboard support
to row grouping and context menus. See
the [Plugins](#/plugins) page for the
full list.

## Using Presets

Assembling plugins one by one gives you full control, but for common setups there are
presets — curated bundles you can use as-is or spread and customize:

```tsx
import { spreadsheet, dataViewer, readOnly } from '@unify/table-react';

// Full spreadsheet — editing, clipboard, keyboard nav, context menus
plugins={spreadsheet()}

// Analytics viewer — filters, grouping, sorting, column resize
plugins={dataViewer()}

// Minimal — filters, column resize, and formatting only
plugins={readOnly()}
```

Since presets return a plain array of plugins, you can compose on top of them:

```tsx
// Start with dataViewer, add context menus
plugins={[...dataViewer(), contextMenu()]}
```

## Choosing a Theme

Unify Table ships with two built-in themes. Each theme object
provides `styles` (CSS class names for the table), `panelVars` (CSS
variable overrides for panels), and `containerClass` (a wrapper class for
scoping). Apply them like this:

```tsx
import { Table, darkTheme, lightTheme } from '@unify/table-react';

const theme = darkTheme; // or lightTheme

<div className={theme.containerClass} style={{ ...theme.panelVars }}>
<Table
db={db}
table="trades"
styles={theme.styles}
height={500}
/>
</div>
```

The `containerClass` and `panelVars` are only needed if you use panels
(filter panel, column panel, etc.). For a standalone table, `styles` alone is
enough. See the [Themes](#/themes) page
for customization details.

## Next Steps

Now that you have a table running, here's where to go next:

- [How It Works](#/how-it-works)
— understand the architecture: how DuckDB handles sorting, filtering, and
grouping in SQL so JavaScript only renders the visible viewport

- [Plugins](#/plugins)
— explore all 16 plugins with live examples and API reference

- [Demo](#/demo)
— see a full application with multiple tables, tabs, and all features working together

---

# How It Works

Understand the architecture that makes Unify Table different.

## The Core Idea

Most data table libraries do everything in JavaScript — sorting, filtering, grouping, aggregation.
This works for small datasets but falls apart at scale. Unify Table takes a fundamentally different
approach: **DuckDB does the heavy lifting in SQL,
React only renders what's visible.**

This isn't just a performance optimization — it's a different mental model. Your table is a SQL view.
Sorting is `ORDER BY`. Filtering is `WHERE`. Grouping
is `GROUP BY`. Aggregation is `SUM`/`AVG`/`COUNT`.
All running in a real analytical database engine, in the browser, via WebAssembly.

The result: you can sort a million rows in milliseconds, filter with complex predicates, group and
aggregate across dozens of columns — all without shipping any of that data to a server or writing
custom comparison functions in JavaScript.

## Architecture Layers

The library is split into three layers, each with a clear responsibility. Dependencies only flow downward
— the core layer has zero dependencies and can be used without React.

### Core Layer

`@unify/table-core` is pure TypeScript with zero dependencies. It provides three key
primitives: the **SQL Builder** for constructing
queries with an immutable, chainable API; the **QueryEngine** for
executing queries against any `TableConnection`; and
the **DataSource** for managing reactive state
(sort, filters, groupBy) with microtask-batched updates.

### React Layer

`@unify/table-react` is the rendering layer. It virtualizes rows
with TanStack Virtual so that a table with a million rows only renders the 30-50
that are actually visible. Features are added through plugins — each one is an independent
module with its own lifecycle.

### Charts Layer

`@unify/table-charts` wraps ECharts with SQL-driven chart builders. Every chart
type — bar, line, pie, scatter, histogram, heatmap — starts as a SQL query. DuckDB aggregates the
data into exactly the shape the chart needs, so you never ship raw data to the charting library.

## Plugin Composition

Features are independent plugins, not props on a mega-component. Each plugin — filtering,
selection, editing, clipboard — is its own module with its own state, lifecycle, and bundle weight.
You compose them as an array, paying only for what you use:

```tsx
<Table
db={db}
table="trades"
plugins={[filters(), selection('multi'), editing(), keyboard()]}
/>
```

See the [Plugins](#/plugins) page for the full architecture, lifecycle, and list of all 16 built-in plugins.

## The SQL Pipeline

Here's what happens when a user clicks a column header to sort:

Every interaction follows this same pattern: user action, state change, SQL generation, DuckDB execution,
viewport render. Filtering adds a `WHERE` clause. Grouping adds `GROUP BY`.
Aggregation adds `SUM()`/`AVG()`. The JavaScript layer never touches your data
directly — it only describes what it wants via SQL.

## Zero-Config to Full Control

Unify Table is designed for progressive complexity. Start simple, add sophistication only when you need it.

### Zero config

Two props. Auto-detects columns from the DuckDB table schema. Renders everything with sensible defaults.

```tsx
<Table db={db} table="trades" />
```

### Add features

Opt into specific capabilities by adding plugins. Each one is independent — mix and match freely.

```tsx
<Table
db={db}
table="trades"
plugins={[filters(), selection('multi'), columnResize()]}
/>
```

### Use presets

Presets are curated plugin bundles for common use cases. One function call instead of a dozen.

```tsx
// Full spreadsheet: editing, clipboard, keyboard, selection, context menu, ...
plugins={spreadsheet()}

// Analytics view: filters, grouping, column resize, formatting, ...
plugins={dataViewer()}

// Minimal read-only: filters, column resize, formatting
plugins={readOnly()}
```

### Go headless

Need full control? Use the headless hook to get the complete `TableContext` and render
everything yourself.

```tsx
const ctx = useTableContext({ db, table: "trades", plugins: [...] });

// You get: rows, columns, sort state, filter state, selection,
// and every method to manipulate them. Render however you want.
```

> **Tip: Ready to build?** Start with the [Getting Started](#/getting-started) guide to set up your first table,
or jump straight to the [Demo](#/demo) to see everything in action.

---

# Table Basics

The Table component renders a virtualized data table backed by DuckDB.

The `Table` component is the main entry point for Unify Table. It takes a DuckDB
connection and a table name, queries the schema, and renders a virtualized grid. Everything
else is optional — columns are auto-detected, plugins add features incrementally, and
themes are applied through CSS. A single `&lt;Table db=&#123;db&#125; table="trades" /&gt;` call
is all you need to get a working table.

## Minimal Example

With just `db` and `table`, columns are auto-detected from the DuckDB schema. Here's 10,000 trades rendered with zero configuration:

## Adding Plugins

The minimal table above is static. Add the `filters()` plugin to let users narrow
down the data — notice the filter row that appears below the headers:

## Table Props

", description: "Initial filter input values" },
{ name: "initialHiddenCols", type: "string[]", description: "Columns to hide on first render" },
{ name: "initialColumnOrder", type: "string[]", description: "Initial column display order" },
{ name: "initialColumnWidths", type: "Record", description: "Initial column widths in pixels" },
{ name: "onSortChange", type: "(sort: SortField[]) => void", description: "Callback when sort state changes" },
{ name: "onFilterValuesChange", type: "(values: Record) => void", description: "Callback when filter values change" },
{ name: "onGroupByChange", type: "(cols: string[]) => void", description: "Callback when group-by columns change" },
]}
/>

## How It Works Under the Hood

When you render a `Table`, three layers work together:

- **DataSource** — a reactive state manager that
owns sort, filter, and groupBy state. When any state changes, it emits events and batches
re-queries via `queueMicrotask` to avoid redundant work.

- **QueryEngine** — a thin wrapper around the
DuckDB connection. It translates the DataSource state into SQL, executes queries, and
returns results. All sorting, filtering, grouping, and aggregation happens in SQL — JavaScript
never touches the full dataset.

- **TanStack Virtual** — handles row
virtualization. Only the rows visible in the viewport are rendered to the DOM, so scrolling
through millions of rows is as fast as scrolling through a hundred.

## Table vs useTableContext

The `Table` component is the standard way to render a table — it handles layout,
virtualization, headers, and plugin rendering out of the box. For cases where you need full
control over the rendered output, use the `useTableContext` hook instead. It returns
the complete `TableContext` (data, state, actions) without rendering anything, so you
can build a fully custom UI while still benefiting from the query engine and plugin system.

> **Tip: When to choose which** Use `Table` when the built-in layout works for your use case. Reach
for `useTableContext` when you need a non-standard layout, want to embed table
data in a custom component, or need to coordinate multiple tables sharing the same data source.

---

# Column Definitions

Control how columns are displayed, formatted, and edited.

Column definitions let you customize how each column is displayed, formatted, and behaves.
If you don't provide column definitions, the Table auto-generates them from DuckDB's
schema — detecting types, choosing sensible defaults for alignment and width, and using
the column name as the header label. Custom definitions let you override any of these defaults.

## Auto-Detected Columns

When no `columns` prop is passed, the table queries DuckDB's schema and creates
columns automatically. This is the simplest way to get started — the table inspects every
column in the DuckDB table, infers types, and renders them with sensible defaults.

## Column Types

DuckDB column types map to four rendering modes:

- **string** — renders as plain text,
left-aligned by default. Includes VARCHAR, TEXT, and similar DuckDB types.

- **number** — renders right-aligned by
default. Includes INTEGER, BIGINT, DOUBLE, DECIMAL, and other numeric types. Supports
format strings like `currency` and `compact`.

- **date** — renders as a formatted date
string. Includes DATE, TIMESTAMP, and TIMESTAMPTZ types.

- **boolean** — renders as a visual
indicator. Maps from DuckDB's BOOLEAN type.

## Custom Columns

Pass an array of column definitions to control labels, widths, alignment, and formatting:

```tsx
<Table
db={db}
table="employees"
columns={[
{ field: 'name', label: 'Full Name', width: 200 },
{ field: 'department', label: 'Dept', width: 120 },
{ field: 'salary', label: 'Salary', align: 'right', format: 'currency' },
{ field: 'hire_date', label: 'Hired', format: 'date' },
]}
/>
```

## Editable Columns

## ColumnDef Props

ReactNode", description: "Custom cell render function" },
{ name: "format", type: "string", description: "Format string (currency, date, compact, etc.)" },
{ name: "editor", type: "'text' | 'number' | 'date' | 'select' | 'textarea' | 'checkbox'", description: "Editor type for inline editing" },
{ name: "editorOptions", type: "string[]", description: "Options for select editor" },
{ name: "validate", type: "(value) => true | string", description: "Validation function, returns error string on failure" },
]}
/>

## Formatting: format vs formatting Plugin

There are two ways to format cell values, and they serve different purposes:

- **The `format` prop** on a column
definition controls how values are displayed as text — currency symbols, date formatting,
compact notation. It is static and applies to every cell in the column.

- **The `formatting` plugin** applies
conditional visual styles — background color, text color, bold — based on cell values.
Use it for things like highlighting negative numbers in red or applying threshold-based
color scales.

> **Tip: Rule of thumb** Use `format` to control what text is shown. Use the `formatting` plugin
to control how that text looks based on its value.

---

# Themes

Built-in dark and light themes with full CSS customization.

Unify Table ships with built-in dark and light themes, both available as pure CSS — no
Tailwind required. Themes provide three things: style class names for table slots (header,
row, cell), CSS variable overrides for panel components (filters, columns panel, etc.),
and a container class for scoping the theme boundary. Import theme CSS through the
package sub-paths:

```tsx
import '@unify/table-react/styles';    // panel CSS
import '@unify/table-react/themes';    // theme class definitions
import '@unify/table-react/displays';  // display renderer CSS
import { darkTheme, lightTheme } from '@unify/table-react';
```

## Built-in Themes

Two themes are provided out of the box: `darkTheme` and `lightTheme`.
Pass either to the `Table` component via the `styles` prop, or
spread the theme object to apply all three parts at once.

## Theme Interface

Every theme implements the `Theme` interface. The three fields correspond to
different layers of the table UI:

```tsx
interface Theme {
styles: TableStyles;              // CSS class names for table slots
panelVars: Record<string, string>; // CSS custom properties for panels
containerClass: string;           // Outer container class
}
```

- **styles** — class names applied to the
table header, rows, even rows, cells, and other structural elements. Use these to control
backgrounds, borders, and hover states.

- **panelVars** — CSS custom properties
injected as inline styles on the table container. Panel components (filter panel, columns
panel, context menus) read these variables for their colors. This is the primary
customization mechanism for non-table UI.

- **containerClass** — a class applied to
the outermost wrapper, useful for scoping borders, rounded corners, or shadows.

## CSS Variables

Panel UI components use these CSS custom properties. Override them
in `panelVars` to change colors without writing any CSS files:

```css
--utbl-color-surface        // Background
--utbl-color-surface-alt    // Alternate background
--utbl-color-border         // Border color
--utbl-color-text           // Primary text
--utbl-color-text-secondary // Secondary text
--utbl-color-text-muted     // Muted text
--utbl-color-accent         // Accent / primary color
--utbl-color-input-bg       // Input background
--utbl-color-input-border   // Input border
```

> **Tip: CSS variables are the primary customization mechanism** Rather than overriding individual class names, start by changing CSS variables
in `panelVars`. This ensures consistent theming across all panel components —
filters, column pickers, context menus, and display renderers all read from the same
variable set.

## Creating a Custom Theme

To create a custom theme, define an object matching the `Theme` interface. You
can start from scratch or spread one of the built-in themes and override specific fields.
The example below creates an indigo-themed dark table:

```tsx
const customTheme: Theme = {
styles: {
header: 'bg-indigo-900 text-white',
row: 'hover:bg-indigo-950/20',
rowEven: 'bg-indigo-950/10',
cell: 'border-b border-indigo-800/30',
},
panelVars: {
'--utbl-color-surface': '#1e1b4b',
'--utbl-color-accent': '#818cf8',
},
containerClass: 'rounded-lg border border-indigo-800',
};
```

---

# Density & Layout

Control row height and table sizing.

Density controls the vertical rhythm of the table — row height, cell padding, and font
size all scale together. Choose a density based on how your users interact with the data:
`compact` fits more rows on screen for scanning and comparison, `comfortable` is
a balanced default for general use, and `spacious` gives breathing room for
tables where readability matters more than density.

## Row Density

Three density options control row height: `compact` (28px), `comfortable` (36px, default), `spacious` (48px).

## Height

Set a fixed height with a number (pixels) or CSS string. Virtual scrolling is enabled
automatically via TanStack Virtual. If no height is specified, the table will fill its
parent container.

## Virtual Scrolling

The table uses TanStack Virtual for efficient rendering. Only visible rows are in the DOM,
so even 1M+ rows scroll smoothly. The virtualizer calculates which rows are in view based
on scroll position and row height, renders only those rows, and recycles DOM nodes as you scroll.

> **Info: Performance** Virtual scrolling means the table only renders visible rows regardless of dataset size.
A table with 1 million rows uses the same memory as one with 100 — the DOM footprint
stays constant. Combined with DuckDB handling all sorting and filtering in SQL, the
browser never needs to hold the full dataset in JavaScript memory.

---

# Plugins

Features are composable plugins, not props on a mega-component.

## Why Plugins?

Traditional data tables suffer from prop explosion — hundreds of props on a single component,
each toggling a different behavior. The result is a monolithic API that's hard to learn, hard
to test, and impossible to tree-shake. Every user pays the bundle cost for every feature,
whether they use it or not.

Plugins flip this model. Each feature — filtering, selection, editing, clipboard — lives in its
own isolated module with its own state, its own tests, and its own bundle weight. You compose
the exact set of capabilities you need as an array, and the table assembles them at runtime.
If you never import `editing()`, it never ships to your users.

> **Tip: Tree-shaking by design** Because plugins are standalone functions, bundlers can eliminate unused plugins entirely.
A read-only table with just `filters()` and `columnResize()` ships
a fraction of the code compared to a full spreadsheet setup.

## Plugin Architecture

```tsx
interface TablePlugin {
name: string;
dependencies?: string[];
init?(ctx: TableContext): void | (() => void);
transformQuery?(sql: string): string;
transformColumns?(columns: ResolvedColumn[]): ResolvedColumn[];
transformRows?(rows: Row[]): Row[];
shortcuts?: Record<string, (ctx: TableContext) => void>;
contextMenuItems?: (ctx: TableContext, cell: CellRef) => MenuItem[];
headerContextMenuItems?: (ctx: TableContext, column: ResolvedColumn) => MenuItem[];
renderAbove?(ctx: TableContext): ReactNode;
renderBelow?(ctx: TableContext): ReactNode;
renderFooter?(ctx: TableContext): ReactNode;
renderOverlay?(ctx: TableContext): ReactNode;
}
```

## Composition

```tsx
// Plugins are composed as an array
<Table
db={db}
table="employees"
plugins={[filters(), selection('multi'), columnResize(), contextMenu()]}
/>
```

> **Info: Plugin Lifecycle** Each plugin's `init()` is called with the full `TableContext`.
Return a cleanup function to run when the table unmounts.

## Execution Order

Plugins run in the order they appear in the array. This matters when plugins interact — for
example, `clipboard()` reads from the current selection, so `selection()` should
come first. Similarly, `keyboard()` relies on knowing which cell is selected to
handle arrow-key navigation.

Each plugin can declare a `dependencies` array listing the plugin names it requires.
If a dependency is missing from the plugins list, the system logs a warning at init time so
you can catch misconfiguration early.

> **Info: Dependency example** `clipboard()` declares `dependencies: ['selection']`. If you forget to
include `selection()`, you'll see a console warning explaining which dependency is missing.

## Writing a Custom Plugin

A plugin is a factory function that returns a `TablePlugin` object.
Here's a complete example — a row counter that shows the total in the footer
and logs on data changes:

```tsx
function rowCounter(): TablePlugin {
return {
name: 'rowCounter',

init(ctx) {
// Subscribe to data events — runs after every query
const unsub = ctx.on('data', () => {
console.log('Rows loaded:', ctx.getLatest().totalCount);
});
// Return cleanup to unsubscribe on unmount
return unsub;
},

renderFooter(ctx) {
return (
<div style={{ padding: '4px 12px', fontSize: 12, opacity: 0.7 }}>
{ctx.totalCount} rows{ctx.isLoading ? ' (loading...)' : ''}
</div>
);
},
};
}

// Use it like any built-in plugin
<Table db={db} table="trades" plugins={[rowCounter(), filters()]} />
```

Plugins can also add keyboard shortcuts and context menu items. This example adds
a "Copy row as JSON" option to the right-click menu:

```tsx
function copyRowJson(): TablePlugin {
return {
name: 'copyRowJson',
dependencies: ['selection'],

shortcuts: {
'Ctrl+Shift+J': (ctx) => {
const { activeCell, rows } = ctx.getLatest();
if (activeCell) {
const row = rows[activeCell.rowIndex];
navigator.clipboard.writeText(JSON.stringify(row, null, 2));
}
},
},

contextMenuItems: (ctx, cell) => [
{
label: 'Copy row as JSON',
action: () => {
const row = ctx.rows[cell.rowIndex];
if (row) navigator.clipboard.writeText(JSON.stringify(row, null, 2));
},
},
],
};
}
```

### Key Patterns

- Always **return a cleanup function** from `init()` to prevent memory leaks on unmount.

- Use **`ctx.getLatest()`** in async or deferred callbacks — closures capture a stale `ctx`.

- Declare **`dependencies`** if your plugin reads state from another plugin (e.g. selection).

- `transformColumns` and `transformRows` must return **new arrays** — they are pure transforms, not mutations.

## Available Plugins

{PLUGINS.map((p) => (

{p.name}()
{p.desc}

))}

---

# Filters

Adds a filter input row below headers for column-level filtering.

Filters let users narrow down visible rows using composable SQL predicates. Under the hood,
each filter becomes a WHERE clause — DuckDB handles the actual filtering, not JavaScript.
This means filtering scales to millions of rows with no performance cliff, and the syntax
you type in the filter input maps directly to efficient SQL operations.

> **Info** See filters in action in the [Interactive Demo](#/demo) — try filtering the trades table by ticker, region, or desk.

## Usage

```tsx
import { Table, filters } from '@unify/table-react';

<Table db={db} table="products" plugins={[filters()]} />
```

## Example

Same `trades_sample` table as the [basic example](#/table-basics),
now with `filters()` added. Notice the filter row below the headers.

500 in notional." height={350} />

## Filter Syntax

Type directly in filter inputs. Supported patterns: `&gt; 100`, `50..500` (range), `A,B,C` (one of), `!value` (not equal), `*pattern*` (contains).

## Built-in Predicates

When building filters programmatically, the core library provides composable predicate functions
that generate safe, parameterized SQL. These can be combined with `and()`, `or()`,
and `not()` combinators.

```tsx
import { eq, neq, gt, gte, lt, lte, contains, startsWith,
endsWith, oneOf, between, isNull, isNotNull,
and, or, not, raw } from '@unify/table-core';

// Comparison
eq('status', 'active')        // status = 'active'
neq('status', 'archived')     // status != 'archived'
gt('price', 100)              // price > 100
gte('price', 100)             // price >= 100
lt('quantity', 10)            // quantity < 10
lte('quantity', 10)           // quantity <= 10

// Pattern matching
contains('name', 'corp')     // name ILIKE '%corp%'
startsWith('name', 'A')      // name ILIKE 'A%'
endsWith('email', '.com')    // email ILIKE '%.com'

// Set & range
oneOf('region', ['US', 'EU']) // region IN ('US', 'EU')
between('price', 10, 100)    // price BETWEEN 10 AND 100

// Null checks
isNull('notes')              // notes IS NULL
isNotNull('notes')           // notes IS NOT NULL

// Combinators
and(gt('price', 50), lt('price', 200))
or(eq('status', 'active'), eq('status', 'pending'))
not(isNull('email'))
```

## Programmatic Filtering

Set filters from code via the DataSource's `setFilters()` method, which
accepts an array of filter predicates. This is useful for building custom filter UIs
or applying filters based on external events.

```tsx
import { gt, between, eq, and } from '@unify/table-core';

// Set filters as an array of predicates
ctx.datasource.setFilters([
gt('price', 100),
eq('status', 'active'),
]);

// Combine multiple conditions on the same column
ctx.datasource.setFilters([
between('price', 50, 500),
eq('status', 'active'),
]);

// Clear all filters by passing an empty array
ctx.datasource.setFilters([]);
```

> **Tip: Debounced by default** Filter inputs in the UI are debounced to avoid firing a query on every keystroke.
Programmatic filters applied via `setFilter()` take effect on the next
microtask batch.

---

# Selection

Enables single, multi, or range cell selection with keyboard and mouse support.

The selection plugin supports three modes, each enabling different interaction patterns.
** Single** mode selects one cell at a time — clicking a new cell deselects the
previous one. ** Multi** mode lets users build up a set of individually selected
cells using Ctrl/Cmd+click. ** Range** mode enables rectangular region selection
via Shift+click or click-and-drag, similar to spreadsheet behavior.

Selection state is used by other plugins: `clipboard()` copies from the current
selection, `editing()` activates on the selected cell, and `statusBar()` shows
aggregations for the selected range.

> **Info** See selection in action in the [Interactive Demo](#/demo) — click, Shift+click, and Ctrl+click cells in any table panel.

## Usage

```tsx
import { Table, selection } from '@unify/table-react';

// Multi-select with Ctrl/Cmd click (default)
<Table db={db} table="orders" plugins={[selection()]} />

// Single cell — one cell at a time
<Table db={db} table="orders" plugins={[selection('single')]} />

// Range selection with Shift+click
<Table db={db} table="orders" plugins={[selection('range')]} />
```

## Example

Building on the same `trades_sample` data — this time with `selection('range')`,
`keyboard()` for arrow-key navigation, and `statusBar()` to show aggregations
of the selected cells.

## Selection Modes

`single` selects one cell at a time. `multi` allows toggling
individual cells with Ctrl/Cmd+click. `range` enables rectangular selection
via Shift+click or Shift+arrow keys.

## When to Use Each Mode

> **Tip: Choosing a selection mode** **single** — Best for detail panels or master-detail layouts where clicking a row
shows its details elsewhere. Keeps the interaction simple and unambiguous.
**multi** — Ideal for batch operations like "delete selected rows" or "tag these
items." Users can Ctrl/Cmd+click to cherry-pick non-contiguous cells.
**range** — The default for spreadsheet-like experiences. Supports copy/paste of
rectangular regions and shows aggregate stats (sum, count, average) in the status bar for the
selected area.

---

# Editing

Non-destructive inline cell editing. The source table is never modified — edits live in
a separate overlay and are merged on the fly via a DuckDB view.

The editing plugin turns your table into a spreadsheet. Double-click a cell, press Enter,
or start typing to enter edit mode. Changes follow commit/cancel semantics — press Enter
or Tab to confirm, Escape to discard.

Under the hood, editing is **non-destructive**. The source table remains
immutable — it can be shared by other views, queries, or users without interference.
Each editable table instance gets its own overlay table in DuckDB that stores only the
modified rows. A merge view combines the source with the overlay, and all downstream
features (filters, sort, grouping) operate on this merged result transparently.

## How It Works

When the editing plugin is active, three DuckDB objects are created behind the scenes:

- **Source table** — your original data, never modified by edits

- **Overlay table** — stores only changed, added, or deleted rows

- **Merge view** — a DuckDB VIEW that unions the source with the overlay, excluding deleted rows and preferring overlay versions of edited rows

The existing ViewManager (which handles filters and sort) sits on top of the merge view.
This means filtering, sorting, and grouping all work unchanged — they simply operate on
the merged result instead of the raw table.

## Usage

```tsx
import { Table, editing, keyboard, selection } from '@unify/table-react';

// Basic editing — source table is never modified
<Table db={db} table="employees" plugins={[editing(), keyboard(), selection('range')]} />
```

Tables are read-only by default. Adding the `editing()` plugin enables
non-destructive editing. Pair it with `keyboard()` for Enter/Tab/Escape
controls and `selection('range')` for spreadsheet-like cell selection.

## Options

```tsx
editing({
enabled: true,    // initial editing state (default: true)
undoDepth: 50,    // max undo stack depth (default: 50)
})
```

- `enabled` — whether editing is active on mount. Can be toggled at runtime via the `editing:toggle` event.

- `undoDepth` — how many operations to keep in the undo stack. Oldest operations are dropped when the limit is exceeded.

## Example

Trade data with `editing()`, `keyboard()`, and `selection('range')`.
Column definitions specify the editor type and validation rules for each field.

## Controls

- **Double-click** or **Enter** — start editing the active cell

- **F2** — start editing without clearing the current value

- **Start typing** — replace the cell value and enter edit mode

- **Enter** — confirm the edit and move down

- **Tab** — confirm the edit and move right

- **Escape** — cancel the edit, revert to the previous value

- **Delete** — clear the cell contents

- **Ctrl+Z** / **⌘Z** — undo the last edit

- **Ctrl+Y** / **Ctrl+Shift+Z** / **⌘⇧Z** — redo

## Validation

Column definitions can include a `validate` function that receives the new
value and returns `true` or an error message string. Invalid edits are
rejected and the cell reverts to its previous value.

```tsx
const columns = [
{
field: 'price',
editor: 'number',
validate: (value: unknown) => {
if (typeof value !== 'number' || value < 0)
return 'Price must be a positive number';
return true;
},
},
{
field: 'email',
validate: (value: unknown) =>
typeof value === 'string' && value.includes('@')
? true
: 'Invalid email address',
},
];
```

> **Info: Type-aware editing** The editor adapts its input to the column type. Numeric columns show a number input,
boolean columns toggle directly, and text columns use a standard text field. Type
coercion happens automatically before validation runs.

## Select Editor

Set `editor: 'select'` with `editorOptions` to restrict a cell to a fixed list of values.
Add `editorFreeform: true` to also allow arbitrary values — the input becomes a combobox where
the user can pick from the list or type a new value.

```tsx
const columns = [
// Strict: only the listed values are allowed
{ field: 'status', editor: 'select', editorOptions: ['Open', 'Closed', 'Pending'] },

// Freeform: suggestions from the list, but any value accepted
{ field: 'category', editor: 'select', editorOptions: ['Bug', 'Feature', 'Chore'], editorFreeform: true },
];
```

## Save & Discard

Edits accumulate in the overlay without touching the source table. You control when
(and whether) they are applied:

```tsx
// Save: flush all overlay edits into the source table
ctx.emit('editing:save');

// Discard: clear all pending edits, revert to original data
ctx.emit('editing:discard');

// Toggle: enable/disable editing mode (edits are preserved)
ctx.emit('editing:toggle');
```

- `editing:save` — applies all pending edits, inserts, and deletes to the source table, then clears the overlay. This is the **only** moment the source table is modified.

- `editing:discard` — drops all pending changes and resets to the original data.

- `editing:toggle` — toggles editing mode on/off. When disabled, cells cannot be edited but pending changes remain visible. Toggle back on to resume editing.

> **Warning: Save is explicit** The source table is never modified automatically. You must emit `editing:save` to
persist changes. This makes it safe to use the editing plugin on shared tables — other
consumers see the original data until you explicitly save.

## Undo & Redo

Every edit, row addition, and row deletion is tracked in an operation log. `Ctrl+Z` (`⌘Z` on macOS) undoes
the last operation, `Ctrl+Y` or `Ctrl+Shift+Z` (`⌘⇧Z` on macOS) redoes it. The undo stack supports all operation types — cell
edits, row additions, and row deletions — and correctly restores the previous state including
reverting overlay rows back to the source when all edits to a row are undone.

## Row Operations

Adding and deleting rows also goes through the overlay:

- **Add row** — inserted into the overlay with state `added`. The source table is not modified.

- **Delete row** — marked as `deleted` in the overlay. The source row is hidden from the merge view but still exists in the source table.

- **Delete an added row** — truly removed from the overlay (it never existed in the source).

The right-click context menu includes a **Delete row** action when the editing plugin is active.

## Architecture

The editing plugin uses the `EditOverlay` from `@unify/table-core` and
publishes an `EditingState` to `ctx.editing`. When the plugin is not registered,
`ctx.editing` is `null` and no editing is possible. All edit operations — including
those triggered by clipboard paste or find-and-replace — automatically route through the overlay.

```tsx
// The overlay is lazy — created on first edit, not on mount
// Access the core EditOverlay via @unify/table-core if building custom integrations
import { createEditOverlay } from '@unify/table-core';
import type { EditOverlay, DirtyRow } from '@unify/table-core';
```

---

# Keyboard

Adds keyboard navigation with arrow keys, Tab, Enter, Escape, Delete, and Ctrl shortcuts.

The keyboard plugin makes the table fully navigable without a mouse. Arrow keys move between cells, Tab advances
to the next cell (wrapping to the next row), Enter starts editing, and Escape cancels. It's the glue that connects 
[selection](#/plugins/selection), 
[editing](#/plugins/editing), and 
[clipboard](#/plugins/clipboard) into a cohesive spreadsheet-like experience.

> **Info: Included in spreadsheet preset** The keyboard plugin is included in the `spreadsheet()` preset. When
using `dataViewer()` or `readOnly()`, add it explicitly if you want
keyboard navigation.

## Usage

```tsx
import { Table, keyboard } from '@unify/table-react';

<Table db={db} table="tasks" plugins={[keyboard()]} />
```

## Shortcuts

## Custom Shortcuts

Plugins can register additional keyboard shortcuts via the `shortcuts` property. Each shortcut maps a key combination to a handler that receives the table context.

```tsx
const myPlugin = {
name: 'myPlugin',
shortcuts: {
'Ctrl+Shift+S': (ctx) => saveSnapshot(ctx),
'Ctrl+Shift+E': (ctx) => exportSelection(ctx),
},
};
```

---

# Clipboard

Copy and paste cells in TSV format. Supports pasting from spreadsheets and paste-as-append.

The clipboard plugin enables `Ctrl+C` / `Ctrl+V` for table cells, using tab-separated values (TSV)
as the interchange format. This means you can copy cells from Unify Table and paste them directly into Excel,
Google Sheets, or any spreadsheet — and vice versa. When pasting data that exceeds the current selection,
new rows are appended to the table automatically.

> **Tip: Works best with Selection + Keyboard** Clipboard reads from the current selection. Pair it with 
[selection('multi')](#/plugins/selection) or 
[selection('range')](#/plugins/selection) for rectangular copy/paste, and 
[keyboard()](#/plugins/keyboard) for Ctrl+C/V shortcut handling.

## Usage

```tsx
import { Table, clipboard, selection, keyboard } from '@unify/table-react';

// Full spreadsheet-like copy/paste experience
<Table
db={db}
table="data"
plugins={[selection('range'), keyboard(), clipboard()]}
/>
```

## Example

Select cells below, then press `Ctrl+C` to copy. Open a spreadsheet and press `Ctrl+V` — the data
transfers as tab-separated values.

## Operations

- `Ctrl+C` — copies selected cells as tab-separated values (TSV), compatible with Excel and Google Sheets

- `Ctrl+V` — pastes TSV data into the selected region, matching columns by position

- **Paste-as-append** — when pasted data has more rows than the selection, excess rows are appended to the table via `INSERT INTO`

> **Warning: Secure context required** The Clipboard API requires HTTPS or localhost. Copy/paste will silently fail on plain HTTP connections.

---

# Column Resize

Drag column borders to resize. Double-click to auto-fit column width to content.

## Usage

```tsx
import { Table, columnResize } from '@unify/table-react';

<Table db={db} table="products" plugins={[columnResize()]} />
```

## Example

## Behavior

- **Drag** — a handle appears on the right edge of each column header. Drag to resize in real time.

- **Double-click** — auto-sizes the column to fit its widest visible content.

- **Persist** — use `initialColumnWidths` and `onColumnWidthsChange` to save/restore widths.

## Persisting Widths

```tsx
// Save and restore column widths
<Table
db={db}
table="products"
plugins={[columnResize()]}
initialColumnWidths={{ name: 200, price: 120 }}
onColumnWidthsChange={(widths) => save(widths)}
/>
```

---

# Column Pin

Pin columns to the left or right edge of the table so they remain visible during horizontal scrolling.

## Usage

```tsx
import { Table, columnPin } from '@unify/table-react';

<Table db={db} table="trades" plugins={[columnPin()]} />
```

## Example

## Pinning Columns

Set `pin: 'left'` or `pin: 'right'` on a column definition to pin it.
Pinned columns stay fixed at the edge while the rest of the table scrolls horizontally,
with a subtle shadow to indicate the boundary.

```tsx
const columns = [
{ field: 'ticker', pin: 'left' },
{ field: 'total', pin: 'right' },
];
```

## Context Menu Pinning

Right-click any column header to pin or unpin it at runtime.
Requires both `columnPin()` and 
`contextMenu()` plugins.

> **Tip: When to pin** Pin identifier columns (name, ticker, ID) so users always see which row they're looking at while scrolling through many columns. Combine with 
[columnResize()](/plugins/column-resize) and 
[columnReorder()](/plugins/column-reorder) for full column management.

---

# Column Reorder

Drag-and-drop column headers to reorder columns using the HTML5 Drag and Drop API.

## Usage

```tsx
import { Table, columnReorder } from '@unify/table-react';

<Table db={db} table="employees" plugins={[columnReorder()]} />
```

## Example

## Behavior

Click and drag any column header to move it. A visual indicator shows the drop
position. The new column order is persisted in the table state and reflected
immediately. Works alongside column pinning -- pinned columns maintain their
pinned position.

---

# Context Menu

Right-click menus with nested submenus. Other plugins can contribute menu items.

The context menu plugin provides a right-click menu system for the table. It collects menu
items from all active plugins, so the menu naturally grows as you add capabilities — include
the clipboard plugin and you get Copy/Paste entries; include tableIO and you get Export
options. Each plugin's items are separated visually, and items can nest into submenus for
complex operations.

You can also define fully custom menu items in your own plugins by implementing
the `contextMenuItems` hook, which receives the table context and the cell that
was right-clicked.

## Usage

```tsx
import { Table, contextMenu } from '@unify/table-react';

<Table db={db} table="orders" plugins={[contextMenu()]} />
```

## Example

This example combines `contextMenu()` with `clipboard()`,
`selection()`, `editing()`, and `keyboard()` —
notice how the menu items change based on which plugins are active.

## Custom Menu Items

```tsx
// Plugins contribute items via contextMenuItems
const myPlugin = {
name: 'audit',
contextMenuItems: (ctx, cell) => [
{ label: 'View history', action: () => showHistory(cell) },
{
label: 'Export',
children: [
{ label: 'As CSV', action: () => exportCsv(ctx) },
{ label: 'As JSON', action: () => exportJson(ctx) },
],
},
],
};
```

Menu items from all active plugins are collected and separated automatically.
Items can include nested `children` arrays for submenus.

> **Tip: Built-in items from other plugins** You don't need to wire up common actions manually. When `clipboard()` is active, the
context menu automatically includes Copy and Paste. When `tableIO()` is active,
Export as CSV/JSON/Parquet appears. The context menu acts as a hub for all plugin-provided
actions.

---

# Views

Save and restore view presets that capture filters, sort order, and groupBy configuration.

## Usage

```tsx
import { Table, views } from '@unify/table-react';

<Table db={db} table="sales" plugins={[views()]} />
```

## API

Views are managed through standalone functions that operate on the table context:

```tsx
import { views, saveView, applyView, listViews, serializeViews } from '@unify/table-react';

// Save current state as a named view
saveView('Q1 Summary', ctx);

// List saved view names
const names = listViews(); // ['Q1 Summary', ...]

// Restore a saved view
applyView('Q1 Summary', ctx);

// Serialize all views to JSON (for persistence)
const json = serializeViews();
```

## What Gets Saved

A view preset captures:

- **Filters** — all active column filters and their values

- **Sort** — sort columns and directions

- **Group By** — grouped columns and aggregation functions

- **Column visibility** — which columns are shown or hidden

- **Column order** — the current arrangement of columns

> **Tip: Use case: team dashboards** Create views for common analysis patterns — "Q1 Revenue by Region", "Top Performers", "Overdue Items" — so team members
can switch between them instantly without rebuilding filters and sorts each time.

---

# Table I/O

Import and export table data in CSV, JSON, and Parquet formats with progress events.

## Usage

Add the `tableIO()` plugin to enable the Export panel and programmatic import/export.

```tsx
import { Table, tableIO, filters, selection } from '@unify/table-react';

<Table
db={db}
table="trades"
plugins={[tableIO(), filters(), selection("range")]}
/>
```

## Export

Export the full table or just the selection. CSV, JSON, Parquet, and XLSX formats are
supported. Parquet uses DuckDB's native writer for maximum performance on large datasets.

```tsx
import { tableIO } from '@unify/table-react';

const io = tableIO();
// After mounting, get the handle from the plugin:
const handle = io.getHandle(ctx);

await handle.exportCSV();                          // full table
await handle.exportCSV({ selection: true });       // selected cells only
await handle.exportJSON({ pretty: true });
await handle.exportParquet({ filename: 'data.parquet' });
await handle.exportXLSX({ sheet: 'Q1 Report' });
```

## Import

```tsx
// Import from a File object (CSV, JSON, or Parquet)
await handle.importFile(file);

// Append to existing table instead of replacing
await handle.importFile(file, { append: true });
```

## Progress Events

Large imports and exports emit progress events so you can display a progress
bar or status indicator.

> **Tip: Parquet for Large Datasets** For tables with 100K+ rows, use Parquet export — it's significantly faster and produces smaller files than CSV or JSON. DuckDB handles the serialization natively in WebAssembly.

---

# Find & Replace

Search across the entire table and optionally replace matches. Shows affected row count before committing.

## Usage

```tsx
import { Table, findReplace, editing, keyboard, selection } from '@unify/table-react';

<Table
db={db}
table="trades"
plugins={[findReplace(), editing(), keyboard(), selection("range")]}
/>
```

## Keyboard Shortcuts

`Ctrl+F` / `⌘F` opens the find bar. `Ctrl+H` / `⌘H` opens find and replace.
Press `Escape` to close the bar.

## Search Options

The find bar includes toggles for **case-sensitive** matching and **regular expression** mode.
Use the column dropdown to limit the search to a specific text column, or leave it on "All columns" to search everywhere.
The match count updates in real time as you type.

## Replacing

Click the expand chevron or press `Ctrl+H` / `⌘H` to reveal the replace row.
Type the replacement text and click **Replace** to update only the current match and advance
to the next one, or **Replace All** to update every matching cell at once.
The affected count is displayed before any replacement is committed, so you can
review the scope of the change.

> **Tip: Works with Editing** Find & replace pairs naturally with the `editing` plugin — find highlights matches across the table, and replace uses the editing pipeline to commit changes with undo/redo support.

## Programmatic API

Use the exported helper functions to search and replace from code:

```tsx
import { findInTable, replaceInTable } from '@unify/table-react';

// Count matching rows (searches all text columns)
const count = await findInTable(ctx, 'AAPL');

// Search in a specific column, case-sensitive
const count2 = await findInTable(ctx, 'Trading', 'desk', false, true);

// Replace all occurrences in a column
const affected = await replaceInTable(ctx, 'AAPL', 'GOOG', 'ticker');
```

---

# Formulas

Add computed columns that evaluate SQL expressions on each row. Formula columns are fully reactive &mdash; when you edit a source column, the formula recalculates automatically.

## How It Works

Formula columns are injected as computed expressions in the DuckDB `SELECT` clause. They run server-side inside DuckDB-WASM, so all DuckDB functions, type casts, and `CASE` expressions are available. Formula columns are **read-only** &mdash; they cannot be directly edited since their value is derived from other columns.

Formula columns are visually indicated with a **&Sigma; icon** in the column header. When a formula is marked `editable: true`, it also shows a **pencil icon** to indicate the user can edit its expression at runtime.

## Example

Three formula columns: **PnL/Unit** (editable), **Year** (read-only), and **Margin %** (editable). Editable formulas show both &Sigma; and pencil icons &mdash; double-click them to modify the expression. Try editing source columns (`pnl`, `volume`, `notional`) to see formulas recalculate.

Full spreadsheet experience with three computed columns. **PnL/Unit** and **Margin %** are editable &mdash; double-click to modify the expression. **Year** is read-only. Edit source columns and watch formulas recalculate.</>}
height={480}
/>

## Usage

Pass formula column definitions to the `formulas()` plugin. Each column needs a `name` (the output column name) and an `expression` (any valid DuckDB SQL expression).

```tsx
import { Table, formulas, spreadsheet } from '@unify/table-react';

<Table
db={db}
table="trades"
plugins={[
...spreadsheet(),
formulas({
columns: [
{ name: 'margin', expression: 'ROUND(pnl / NULLIF(notional, 0) * 100, 2)', label: 'Margin %', editable: true },
{ name: 'trade_year', expression: "DATE_PART('year', trade_date)::INTEGER", label: 'Year' },  // read-only (default)
],
}),
]}
/>
```

## Column Options

Each `FormulaColumnDef` supports:

```tsx
interface FormulaColumnDef {
name: string;         // Output column name (used as field)
expression: string;   // DuckDB SQL expression
label?: string;       // Display label (defaults to name)
width?: number;       // Column width in px (default: 150)
align?: 'left' | 'center' | 'right';
format?: string;      // Number format (e.g. 'number', 'currency', 'percent')
editable?: boolean;   // Allow users to edit the expression at runtime (default: false)
}
```

## Editing + Formulas

When combined with the editing plugin (or the `spreadsheet()` preset), formula columns are **automatically recalculated** when you edit any source column. The formula expressions run inside DuckDB's view, so edits to the overlay table are reflected immediately on commit.

## Editing Expressions

Formula columns marked with `editable: true` allow users to modify the SQL expression at runtime. Double-click or press **Enter** / **F2** on an editable formula cell to open an inline editor showing the raw SQL expression. Non-editable formulas (the default) cannot be opened for editing.

- **Enter** commits the new expression &mdash; the entire column recalculates instantly

- **Escape** cancels without changes

- **Ctrl+Z** / **Ctrl+Y** undo and redo expression changes (falls back to formula undo when there are no cell edits to undo)

- If the expression is invalid SQL, the change is rolled back and the editor stays open

> **Info: Column-Level Edit** Editing a formula cell changes the expression for the **entire column**, not just one row. This is because formula expressions are DuckDB SQL that applies uniformly to all rows. Source columns (PnL, Volume, etc.) can still be edited per-cell as normal.

## Fill Handle

The `fillHandle()` plugin (included in the `spreadsheet()` preset) adds an Excel-like fill handle &mdash; a small blue square at the bottom-right of the active cell. Drag it to extend values with pattern detection:

- **Numbers**: detects arithmetic progressions (1, 2, 3 &rarr; 4, 5, 6)

- **Dates**: detects intervals and extrapolates

- **Text**: copies or cycles through source values

- **Formula columns**: skipped (they recalculate from source data)

You can also use keyboard shortcuts: **Ctrl+D** (Fill Down) and **Ctrl+R** (Fill Right) when a range is selected. Double-click the handle to auto-fill down to the extent of the adjacent column.

## Expressions

Any valid DuckDB SQL expression works &mdash; built-in functions, `CASE` statements, arithmetic, type casts, and string functions.

```tsx
// Arithmetic
{ name: 'total', expression: 'price * quantity' }

// Conditional logic
{ name: 'status', expression: "CASE WHEN pnl > 0 THEN 'Profit' ELSE 'Loss' END" }

// Date functions
{ name: 'quarter', expression: "DATE_PART('quarter', trade_date)::INTEGER" }

// String functions
{ name: 'ticker_lower', expression: 'LOWER(ticker)' }

// Null-safe division
{ name: 'ratio', expression: 'ROUND(a / NULLIF(b, 0), 4)' }
```

> **Tip: DuckDB Functions** You have access to the full DuckDB function library &mdash; `DATE_PART`, `ROUND`, `NULLIF`, `CASE WHEN`, `COALESCE`, string functions, and more. Check the DuckDB documentation for available functions.

---

# Row Grouping

Group rows by one or more columns with expand/collapse and aggregation support.

Row grouping lets users collapse rows into hierarchical groups by one or more columns.
Combined with aggregations, it transforms a flat table into a pivot-like view — all powered
by GROUP BY queries in DuckDB. This means grouping and aggregation run at database speed,
not in JavaScript, so the experience stays snappy even on large datasets.

Group by a single column to see totals per category, or nest multiple columns for a
drilldown hierarchy (e.g. region, then product, then quarter). Each group header row
shows the aggregated values and can be expanded to reveal the underlying rows.

> **Info** See row grouping in action in the [Interactive Demo](#/demo) — the "Trades" panel groups by region with PnL aggregations.

## Usage

```tsx
import { Table, rowGrouping } from '@unify/table-react';

<Table db={db} table="sales" plugins={[rowGrouping()]} />
```

## Example

## Aggregations

Aggregations are configured when creating the plugin. Each entry specifies a column
and an aggregation function. The aggregated values appear in the group header rows.

```tsx
import { Table, rowGrouping } from '@unify/table-react';

<Table
db={db}
table="sales"
plugins={[
rowGrouping({
aggregations: [
{ field: 'revenue', fn: 'sum' },
{ field: 'quantity', fn: 'avg' },
{ field: 'orders', fn: 'count' },
],
defaultExpanded: false,
}),
]}
initialGroupBy={['region', 'product']}
/>
```

## Expand / Collapse

Group header rows show aggregated values and can be expanded or collapsed.
All grouping and aggregation happens in SQL via DuckDB GROUP BY, so performance
scales to millions of rows.

> **Info: Available aggregations** Built-in aggregation functions include `sum`, `avg`, `count`, 
`min`, `max`, `count_distinct`, `median`, 
`first`, `last`, `mode`, `stddev`, 
`variance`, and `string_agg`. You can also register custom aggregations
via `registerAgg()`.

---

# Formatting

Conditional cell formatting with threshold, negative, and positive value rules.

## Usage

```tsx
import { Table, formatting } from '@unify/table-react';

<Table db={db} table="portfolio" plugins={[formatting()]} />
```

## Example

## Formatting Rules

Pass a rules map to `formatting()` at creation time. Each key is a column
field name (or `'*'` for all columns), and the value is an array
of `ConditionalRule` objects. Three helpers produce common rules:

```tsx
import { formatting, positive, negative, threshold } from '@unify/table-react';

<Table
db={db}
table="portfolio"
plugins={[
formatting({
pnl: [...positive('#22c55e'), ...negative('#ef4444')],
notional: threshold([
{ max: 100000, style: { color: '#f59e0b' } },
{ max: 500000, style: { color: '#22c55e', fontWeight: 'bold' } },
]),
}),
]}
/>
```

## Rule Types

`negative(color?)` applies when the cell value is &lt; 0. 
`positive(color?)` applies when &gt; 0. 
`threshold(ranges)` applies styles based on value ranges.
For custom logic, write a rule directly with a `when` function:

```tsx
formatting({
status: [
{ when: (value) => value === 'Active', style: { color: '#22c55e' } },
{ when: (value) => value === 'Inactive', style: { color: '#ef4444' } },
],
})
```

---

# Status Bar

Enriches the table footer with selection-based aggregations like sum, average, count, min, and max.

## Usage

```tsx
import { Table, statusBar } from '@unify/table-react';

<Table db={db} table="transactions" plugins={[statusBar()]} />
```

## Example

## Behavior

When cells are selected, the footer displays aggregation values computed from
the selected range. For numeric columns this includes sum, average, min, and max.
For all columns, the count of selected cells is shown. Aggregations update
reactively as the selection changes.

---

# Presets

Pre-configured plugin bundles for common use cases.

The plugin pages showed how to compose features one by one. Presets bundle them for common
use cases — instead of assembling plugins individually, pick a preset and customize from
there. Each preset returns a standard plugin array, so you can spread it and add or override
individual plugins as needed.

> **Tip: Start with a preset, then customize** Presets are just functions that return `TablePlugin[]`. You can use the spread
operator to extend them: `[...spreadsheet(), myCustomPlugin()]`.

## Comparison

Each preset targets a different use case. The table below shows which plugins are included in each.

Plugin
spreadsheet()
dataViewer()
readOnly()

{PRESET_PLUGINS.map((row) => (

{row.plugin}
{[row.spreadsheet, row.dataViewer, row.readOnly].map((val, i) => (

{val === false ? "\u2014" : val === true ? "\u2713" : typeof val === "string" ? val : "\u2713"}

))}

))}

## spreadsheet()

Full editing experience: editing, clipboard, keyboard, filters, context menu, selection (range), column resize/reorder, formatting, status bar.

## dataViewer()

Analytics-oriented: filters, selection (multi), column resize/reorder, row grouping, views, formatting, status bar.

## readOnly()

Minimal: filters, column resize, formatting. No editing, no selection.

## Custom Presets

```tsx
// Create your own preset by combining plugins
function myPreset(): TablePlugin[] {
return [
filters(),
selection('multi'),
columnResize(),
rowGrouping(),
statusBar(),
];
}
```

---

# Panels

Side panels provide UI for filtering, grouping, column management, and more.

Panels are slide-out UI components that provide configuration interfaces — filter builder, column manager, group-by picker, and more. Click the icons in the toolbar to open each panel. Only one panel is open at a time.

## Built-in Panels

Six panels ship with the table, each tied to a specific plugin or feature:

**Filters** — visual filter builder with per-column predicates and combinators. Requires the `filters` plugin.
**Group By** — drag-and-drop column grouping with aggregation configuration. Requires the `rowGrouping` plugin.
**Columns** — show, hide, reorder, and pin columns. Works with the `columnReorder` and `columnPin` plugins.
**Export** — export table data to CSV, JSON, or Parquet. Requires the `tableIO` plugin.
**Displays** — switch between display modes (chart, stats, pivot, etc.) and configure display options.
**Debug** — inspect the current SQL query, state, and performance metrics during development.

## Panel Configuration

Panels are enabled by default. You can disable them entirely or change their position relative to the table.

```tsx
// Disable panels entirely
<Table panels={false} />

// Panel position
<Table panelPosition="left" />  // or "right" (default)
```

## Custom Panels

Register your own panels alongside the built-in ones. Each descriptor specifies a key, label, optional icon, and a render function that receives the full `TableContext`.

```tsx
interface PanelDescriptor {
key: string;
label: string;
icon?: React.ComponentType<{ size?: number }>;
render: (ctx: TableContext) => ReactNode;
}
```

> **Tip: Panel + Plugin Pattern** Custom panels work best when paired with a custom plugin. The plugin manages state and logic, while the panel provides the UI. This keeps your panel component thin and testable.

---

# Displays

Alternative visualizations for your table data. Switch between table view and displays.

> **Info** See all display types in action in the [Interactive Demo](#/demo) — each panel showcases a different display mode.

## When to Use Displays

Displays transform the same underlying data into different visual formats — without leaving the table context.
Switch from a data grid to a chart, stats summary, or pivot table with a single click. Because displays
share the same data source, any active filters, sorts, or groupings carry over automatically.

> **Tip: One data source, many views** Displays are not separate pages or routes. They are alternative renderings of the same query result.
This means you can filter your data in table view, then flip to a chart to visualize the filtered subset.

## Two-Layer Architecture

Each display is split into two layers that can be used independently:

- **Core `DisplayType`** (in `@unify/table-core`) — defines the SQL builder,
default config, and validation logic. This layer has zero dependencies and can run in any JS environment.

- **React `DisplayDescriptor`** (in `@unify/table-react`) — provides the
rendering component, config UI panel, and icon. This layer depends on React and the charts package.

This separation means you can use the SQL builders from core to generate display queries server-side,
or build your own rendering layer on top of the core types.

## Adding Displays

Pass an array of display definitions to the `displays` prop. Each display needs a unique `id`,
a `type` matching one of the seven built-in displays, and a `config` object specific to that type.

```tsx
<Table
db={db}
table="orders"
plugins={dataViewer()}
displays={[
{
id: 'my-chart',
type: 'chart',
label: 'Revenue Chart',
config: { type: 'bar', x: 'category', y: { field: 'amount', agg: 'sum' } },
},
]}
/>
```

## Writing a Custom Display

Custom displays follow the same two-layer pattern as built-ins. Define a core `DisplayType` (SQL
builder, config, validation), then a React `DisplayDescriptor` (rendering + config UI).

### Step 1 — Core type

The core type has no React dependency — it generates SQL and defines configuration. This example
creates a "Top N" display that shows the highest values in a column as cards.

```tsx
import { DisplayType, quoteIdent } from '@unify/table-core';

interface TopNConfig {
field: string;
labelField: string;
agg: 'sum' | 'avg' | 'max';
limit: number;
}

const topNDisplayType: DisplayType<TopNConfig> = {
key: 'top-n',
label: 'Top N',
description: 'Show the N highest values',

buildSql(viewName, config) {
return \`SELECT \${quoteIdent(config.labelField)} AS label,
\${config.agg.toUpperCase()}(\${quoteIdent(config.field)}) AS value
FROM \${quoteIdent(viewName)}
GROUP BY \${quoteIdent(config.labelField)}
ORDER BY value DESC LIMIT \${config.limit}\`;
},

defaultConfig(columns) {
const num = columns.find(c => c.mappedType === 'number');
const str = columns.find(c => c.mappedType === 'string');
return { field: num?.name ?? '', labelField: str?.name ?? '', agg: 'sum', limit: 5 };
},

validate(config) {
if (!config.field) return ['Value field is required'];
if (!config.labelField) return ['Label field is required'];
return null;
},
};
```

### Step 2 — React descriptor

The descriptor provides the render component and config panel. Use `useDisplayData(sql, engine)` to
fetch results from the SQL the core type generated.

```tsx
import { registerDisplayType, registerDisplay, useDisplayData } from '@unify/table-react';
import type { DisplayRenderProps, DisplayConfigProps } from '@unify/table-react';

function TopNRender({ sql, engine }: DisplayRenderProps<TopNConfig>) {
const { rows, isLoading } = useDisplayData(sql, engine);
if (isLoading) return <div>Loading...</div>;
return (
<div style={{ display: 'flex', gap: 8, flexWrap: 'wrap', padding: 12 }}>
{rows.map((r, i) => (
<div key={i} style={{ padding: '8px 16px', borderRadius: 8, border: '1px solid #333' }}>
<div style={{ fontSize: 18, fontWeight: 700 }}>{String(r.value)}</div>
<div style={{ fontSize: 12, opacity: 0.6 }}>{String(r.label)}</div>
</div>
))}
</div>
);
}

function TopNConfigPanel({ config, onChange, columns }: DisplayConfigProps<TopNConfig>) {
const numCols = columns.filter(c => c.mappedType === 'number');
const strCols = columns.filter(c => c.mappedType === 'string');
return (
<div style={{ display: 'flex', flexDirection: 'column', gap: 8 }}>
<label>Value field:
<select value={config.field} onChange={e => onChange({ ...config, field: e.target.value })}>
{numCols.map(c => <option key={c.name}>{c.name}</option>)}
</select>
</label>
<label>Label field:
<select value={config.labelField} onChange={e => onChange({ ...config, labelField: e.target.value })}>
{strCols.map(c => <option key={c.name}>{c.name}</option>)}
</select>
</label>
</div>
);
}

// Register both layers — call once at app startup
registerDisplayType(topNDisplayType);
registerDisplay({ type: topNDisplayType, render: TopNRender, renderConfig: TopNConfigPanel });
```

### Step 3 — Use it

```tsx
<Table
db={db}
table="trades"
displays={[{
id: 'top-tickers',
type: 'top-n',
label: 'Top Tickers by PnL',
config: { field: 'pnl', labelField: 'ticker', agg: 'sum', limit: 5 },
}]}
/>
```

**Core layer** defines the SQL — testable without React. 
**React layer** renders results and provides a config panel. 
`useDisplayData(sql, engine)` handles fetching, loading state, and caching. 
`validate()` prevents broken queries from running.

## Choosing a Display

Each display type is designed for a specific analytical task. Use the guide below to pick the right one.

Display
Best for
Example

{USE_CASE_GUIDE.map((row) => (

{row.display}
{row.useCase}
{row.example}

))}

## Available Displays

{DISPLAYS.map((d) => (

{d.name}
{d.desc}

))}

---

# Chart Display

10 chart types: bar, line, area, pie, donut, scatter, histogram, heatmap, treemap, funnel.

The chart display renders your table data as interactive ECharts visualizations. It supports bar, line,
area, pie, donut, scatter, histogram, heatmap, treemap, and funnel charts — all driven by SQL
aggregation queries that DuckDB executes before the data reaches the browser.

> **Info** See charts in action in the [Interactive Demo](#/demo) — the Volume Chart and PnL Chart panels use bar and line displays.

## Chart Type Guide

- **Bar** — compare discrete categories (revenue by region, counts by status)

- **Line** — show trends over an ordered axis (daily sales, monthly growth)

- **Area** — like line, but emphasizes volume (cumulative totals, stacked contributions)

- **Pie / Donut** — show proportions of a whole (market share, category breakdown)

- **Scatter** — reveal relationships between two numeric variables (price vs. quantity)

- **Histogram** — visualize the distribution of a single numeric column (order amounts)

- **Heatmap** — show density or intensity across two dimensions (hour vs. day activity)

- **Treemap** — display hierarchical proportions (nested category breakdowns)

- **Funnel** — show sequential drop-off stages (conversion pipeline, process stages)

## Bar Chart

## Configuration

```tsx
{
type: 'bar',       // bar | line | area | pie | donut | scatter | histogram | heatmap | treemap | funnel
x: 'category',    // X-axis field
y: { field: 'amount', agg: 'sum' },  // Y-axis: field + aggregation
series: 'region',  // Optional: group by color
sort: 'value',     // Sort by value or name
horizontal: false, // Horizontal bars
stacked: false,    // Stack series
limit: 5000,       // Max data points
}
```

> **Info: Performance limit** Charts default to a `LIMIT 5000` on the aggregation query to keep rendering fast.
You can increase this value in the config, but large point counts may cause browser lag with
interactive features like tooltips and zoom.

---

# Stats Display

Summary statistic cards with aggregations per column. Compact notation (K/M/B/T) for large numbers.

The stats display renders summary cards — one per aggregation — with compact number notation (K/M/B/T).
Ideal for dashboards where you need quick KPIs at a glance. Each card runs a single SQL aggregation
against the current (potentially filtered) data, so the numbers always reflect what the user sees.

> **Info** See stats in action in the [Interactive Demo](#/demo) — the "Stats" panel shows aggregated trade metrics.

## Stats Cards

## Configuration

```tsx
{
fields: [
{ field: 'amount', agg: 'sum', label: 'Total Revenue' },
{ field: 'amount', agg: 'avg', label: 'Avg Order' },
{ field: 'id', agg: 'count', label: 'Order Count' },
{ field: 'amount', agg: 'max', label: 'Largest Order' },
],
layout: 'grid',  // grid | row | column
}
```

## Supported Aggregations

```tsx
// Built-in aggregations:
// sum, avg, count, min, max, count_distinct,
// median, first, last, any, mode, stddev, variance, string_agg

// Custom aggregations via registerAgg():
import { registerAgg } from '@unify/table-core';
registerAgg('p95', (field) => \`PERCENTILE_CONT(0.95) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY \${field})\`);
```

---

# Pivot Display

Cross-tabulation matrix with GROUP BY and client-side pivoting. Supports row and column totals.

The pivot display creates a cross-tabulation matrix from your data. Rows become one dimension,
columns become another, and cells show aggregated values. It is the SQL `GROUP BY` equivalent
of a spreadsheet pivot table — the grouping and aggregation happen in DuckDB, then the result is
pivoted client-side into a matrix layout with optional row and column totals.

## Pivot Table

## Configuration

```tsx
{
rowField: 'region',       // Row grouping field
colField: 'category',     // Column grouping field
valueField: 'amount',     // Value to aggregate
agg: 'sum',               // Aggregation function
showRowTotals: true,      // Show total column on the right
showColTotals: true,      // Show total row at the bottom
}
```

## Styling

Pivot tables use `utbl-pivot-*` CSS classes for styling. Override them to customize appearance.

```css
.utbl-pivot-cell { padding: 4px 8px; text-align: right; }
.utbl-pivot-header { font-weight: 600; }
.utbl-pivot-total { background: var(--pivot-total-bg); }
```

---

# Summary Display

Auto-profile every column via DuckDB `SUMMARIZE`. Lazy-loaded histograms per card.

The summary display auto-profiles every column in your table using DuckDB's `SUMMARIZE` command.
Each column gets a card showing distribution, null rate, unique count, and a lazy-loaded histogram.
This is the fastest way to understand a new dataset — no configuration required.

## Column Profiles

## How It Works

The summary display runs DuckDB's built-in `SUMMARIZE` statement to compute per-column statistics including min, max, unique count, null count, and approximate quantiles. Each column card lazily loads a histogram when scrolled into view.

## Configuration

```tsx
{
// Summary display uses auto-detection, minimal config needed.
// Identity columns (id, *_id) are excluded by default.
excludeColumns: ['internal_notes'],  // Additional columns to exclude
}
```

## Output Per Column

```tsx
// Each column card shows:
// - Column name and type
// - Min / Max values
// - Unique count
// - Null count and percentage
// - Approximate quartiles (numeric columns)
// - Histogram (lazy-loaded on scroll)
```

---

# Correlation Display

Pairwise Pearson correlation heatmap for numeric columns using DuckDB `CORR()`.

The correlation display computes pairwise Pearson correlation coefficients between all numeric columns,
rendered as an ECharts heatmap. Use it to quickly identify relationships between variables — for example,
whether discount percentage correlates with order value, or if two metrics move together.

## Correlation Matrix

## How It Works

The display automatically detects numeric columns, computes pairwise `CORR()` values via SQL, and renders an ECharts heatmap. Index-based aliases (`p_i_j`) keep the SQL compact for large column sets.

## Configuration

```tsx
{
// Correlation display auto-detects numeric columns.
// Identity columns (id, *_id) are excluded by default.
columns: ['price', 'quantity', 'discount', 'total'],  // Optional: explicit column list
}
```

## Interpreting Results

```tsx
// Correlation values range from -1 to 1:
//  1.0  = perfect positive correlation
//  0.0  = no linear correlation
// -1.0  = perfect negative correlation
//
// Color scale: red (negative) -> white (zero) -> blue (positive)
```

---

# Timeline Display

Time-bucketed chart using `date_trunc`. Reuses `buildBarLineOption` from the charts package.

The timeline display buckets your data by time intervals (day, week, month, quarter, year) and renders
a time-series chart. Ideal for understanding how metrics evolve over time — pick a date column,
choose a bucket size, and the display generates a `date_trunc`-based SQL query that groups
and aggregates your data into uniform time periods.

> **Info** See the timeline in action in the [Interactive Demo](#/demo) — the "PnL Timeline" panel shows trade PnL over time.

## Time Series

## Configuration

```tsx
{
dateField: 'created_at',      // Date/timestamp column
bucket: 'month',              // year | quarter | month | week | day | hour
valueField: 'amount',         // Value to aggregate
agg: 'sum',                   // Aggregation function
chartType: 'bar',             // bar | line | area
}
```

## Date Bucketing

The SQL builder uses DuckDB's `date_trunc` function to bucket timestamps into uniform intervals. This ensures consistent spacing even when data has gaps.

```sql
-- Generated SQL for monthly bucketing:
SELECT date_trunc('month', "created_at") AS bucket,
SUM("amount") AS value
FROM "orders"
GROUP BY bucket
ORDER BY bucket
```

---

# Outliers Display

IQR and z-score outlier detection with inline SVG box plot and outlier data table.

The outliers display detects statistical outliers using either IQR (Interquartile Range) or z-score
methods. Results are shown as an inline SVG box plot alongside a table of outlier records. This is
useful for data quality checks — quickly surface unusually large orders, price spikes, or any
values that fall outside the expected range.

## Outlier Detection

## Configuration

```tsx
{
field: 'amount',          // Numeric field to analyze
method: 'iqr',            // iqr | zscore
threshold: 1.5,           // IQR multiplier (default 1.5) or z-score cutoff (default 3)
}
```

## IQR Method

The interquartile range method flags values below `Q1 - 1.5 * IQR` or above `Q3 + 1.5 * IQR`.
A CTE computes quartile stats, then a sentinel `UNION ALL` row (wrapped in a subquery) carries the box plot statistics.

## Z-Score Method

The z-score method flags values where `|value - mean| / stddev &gt; threshold`. Default threshold is 3 standard deviations.

```sql
-- Generated SQL for IQR detection:
WITH stats AS (
SELECT PERCENTILE_CONT(0.25) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY "amount") AS q1,
PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY "amount") AS q3
FROM "orders"
),
bounds AS (
SELECT q1, q3, (q3 - q1) AS iqr,
q1 - 1.5 * (q3 - q1) AS lower,
q3 + 1.5 * (q3 - q1) AS upper
FROM stats
)
SELECT * FROM "orders"
WHERE "amount" < (SELECT lower FROM bounds)
OR "amount" > (SELECT upper FROM bounds)
```

---

# Charts

ECharts wrapper and SQL chart builders from `@unify/table-charts`.

The charts package wraps Apache ECharts with SQL-driven data builders. Query the same `trades_sample` data as the table examples, then pass the results through an option builder to get a complete ECharts config.

## Available Chart Types

Each chart type has a dedicated option builder and, where applicable, a SQL builder that generates the aggregation query.

**Bar / Line / Area** — categorical or time-series data with optional stacking. SQL builder: `barLineSql`.
**Pie / Donut** — proportional data with optional series for nested rings. SQL builder: `pieSql`.
**Scatter** — two numeric axes for correlation analysis. SQL builder: `scatterSql`.
**Histogram** — distribution of a single numeric column with configurable bin count. SQL builder: `histogramSql`.
**Heatmap** — two-dimensional density using color intensity. SQL builder: `heatmapSql`.
**Treemap** — hierarchical proportional areas. Config-only (no dedicated SQL builder).
**Funnel** — sequential stage drop-off. Config-only (no dedicated SQL builder).

## Option Builders

Option builders transform query results into ECharts option objects. Pass the data rows and a chart config, and get back a complete option ready to render.

```tsx
import { buildBarLineOption, buildPieOption, buildScatterOption,
buildHistogramOption, buildHeatmapOption, buildTreemapOption,
buildFunnelOption, DARK_CHART_THEME } from '@unify/table-charts';

// Query data from DuckDB
const data = await db.runAndRead(
'SELECT ticker, SUM(pnl) AS total_pnl FROM trades GROUP BY ticker'
);

// Build the ECharts option
const option = buildBarLineOption(data, {
type: 'bar',
x: 'ticker',
y: { field: 'total_pnl', agg: 'sum' },
theme: DARK_CHART_THEME,
});
```

## EChartsWrapper

A thin React component that manages the ECharts instance lifecycle — initialization, resizing, theme changes, and disposal. Pass any ECharts option object.

```tsx
import { EChartsWrapper } from '@unify/table-charts';

<EChartsWrapper option={option} style={{ width: '100%', height: 400 }} />
```

## Sparkline

A minimal inline chart for embedding in table cells, cards, or tight layouts. Renders a tiny line or bar chart with no axes, labels, or interactivity.

```tsx
import { Sparkline } from '@unify/table-charts';

<Sparkline data={[10, 20, 15, 30, 25]} type="line" width={80} height={24} />
```

> **Tip: Standalone vs Display System** For quick one-off charts, use `EChartsWrapper` directly with an option builder. For charts that live alongside your table data and respond to the table's filters and grouping, use the "chart" display type — it handles SQL generation, data fetching, and a configuration UI automatically.

---

# SQL Builder

Immutable, chainable SQL builder with four statement types.

The SQL builder is the foundation of Unify Table. It's an immutable, chainable API that generates safe, properly escaped SQL from TypeScript. Every sort, filter, and group-by operation in the table ultimately becomes a SQL query built by this layer.

Each method call returns a new builder instance, so you can safely branch queries without mutating shared state. This makes it easy to build base queries and extend them for different purposes.

> **Info: Used internally by Table** You don't need to use the SQL builder to render a table — the `&lt;Table&gt;` component
and its [DataSource](#/query-engine) use it internally
to generate all queries. Reach for the builder directly when you need custom queries outside the
table lifecycle, such as dashboards, reports, or data transformations.

## Why a SQL Builder?

Instead of reimplementing sort, filter, and aggregate logic in JavaScript, Unify Table delegates to DuckDB — a real analytical database. The SQL builder ensures every query is properly escaped and optimized.
